title: Hello, World
excerpt: First post — an intro to what I plan to write about here: Linux, cloud support, development, and the lessons learned from keeping production systems alive.
content: Welcome to my blog. I've been meaning to start one of these for a while, and here we are.
Most of what I'll be writing about comes from my day-to-day work as a Senior Technical Support Engineer — that means Linux, cloud infrastructure, debugging things that should not be broken, and more.
Expect posts on troubleshooting methodology, interesting bugs I've encountered, tools I rely on, and the kind of institutional knowledge that never makes it into documentation.
If any of it is useful to you, great. If it's just useful to me as a way to think through problems in writing, that's fine too.

title: My Take on the Popularity of AI
excerpt: The website and the first post were written by AI.
content: Three years ago, I went out of my way to avoid using AI. I literally would move from 'AI search' to regular search results, if AI was enabled. I was still teaching classes at my local college, and I saw the results of students using AI to try and shortcut through programming and web fundamentals. In my short time teaching, I quickly learned to think of AI as cheating.

However, when I moved on to a new job, I found myself utilizing AI more and more. It started small with reading what Google's AI results would say. If I had put enough context into the search prompt, it was generally good information. Eventually, I faced issues at work that none of us had seen before, and I found myself using AI more to gain insights into the problems I was facing. First, I used it to help guide me in research. Soon, it was helping to organize my RCA documentation. Sometimes it would include details I had not thought about. By the end of my first support role, it was analyzing Java flight recordings for me.

Now, as I look for a new position since being laid off, I needed to improve my resume and create a personal website. I decided to pay for Claude and try it out. It wrote my website with a single prompt. It personalized it with a second prompt and attached resume. I only needed to tweak a single line. Later, it created this blog page with a third prompt. I was amazed that it could do all this in minutes.

Granted, this site is extraordinarily simple compared to production solutions. But it still shows how powerful AI can be for development. I can only imagine how much more powerful it will be in the future, and I'm excited to see where it goes.
